questions.prompt: A provider's status change causes a webhook storm — dozens of unrelated events fire. Without protections, what happens?

choices.text: The assistant pushes a flood of notifications to every user. Without per-event circuit breakers, rate limits, and dedup, a single upstream incident creates a notification storm. The fix: circuit breaker (stop firing after N events/min for a single source), rate limit per user (max 3 push/hour), dedup, and alerting on storm patterns.

questions.explanation: Event firehoses are real. Defend with circuit breakers, rate limits, and dedup. Surface storm alerts to engineering so you catch and pause before user trust evaporates.

questions.prompt: When an event-triggered notification fires, what should you always show the user?

choices.text: What triggered it — 'Triggered by: new email from Sarah (VIP rule)' or 'Triggered by: calendar conflict at 2pm'. Users distrust unexplained interruptions; surfacing the trigger ties the notification to a rule they understand and can edit. Builds trust over time.

questions.explanation: Surfacing the trigger is part of the trust UX. Users learn 'when this notification arrives, here's why' — they can adjust the rule, ignore intentionally, or appreciate the catch. Hidden triggers feel arbitrary.
